summary:
Ashoka the Great (r. 268–232 BC) was the third emperor of the [[event:74|Maurya Empire (India)]] and one of history's most remarkable rulers — a conqueror who renounced war after witnessing its devastation, converted to Buddhism, and attempted to govern his vast empire on principles of dharma (moral law), nonviolence, and religious tolerance. His example and the spread of Buddhism he facilitated shaped the religious and cultural history of Asia more profoundly than almost any other individual.

Ashoka came to power after a brutal succession struggle and initially ruled as a conventional conqueror. The [[event:1099|Kalinga War]] (261 BC), in which he defeated the kingdom of Kalinga (modern Odisha) at the cost of perhaps 100,000 lives, produced in him a reported transformation of conscience. According to the [[event:1100|Edicts of Ashoka]], he was overwhelmed by remorse at the suffering the conquest had caused. He converted to Buddhism, though he did not impose it — his [[event:1100|Edicts of Ashoka]] explicitly advocate tolerance of all religious traditions — and declared that he would henceforth conquer not by arms but by dharma.

The Edicts of Ashoka, inscribed on rocks and polished sandstone pillars across the empire and in several languages and scripts, are the first substantial historical inscriptions from South Asia and a remarkable primary source. They record policies of animal welfare (reducing sacrifice, banning hunting on certain days), medical facilities for humans and animals along roads, planting of trees and digging of wells, humane treatment of prisoners, religious tolerance, and a general ethic of compassion. The pillar capitals — most famously the Lion Capital of Ashoka, now India's national emblem — are masterpieces of Mauryan art.

The [[event:1101|Spread of Buddhism under Ashoka]] was one of history's most consequential religious transmissions. Ashoka reportedly sent Buddhist missionaries to all parts of the empire and beyond — to the Greek kingdoms of the west, to Southeast Asia, and most importantly to Sri Lanka, where his son Mahinda converted the island's king and established Theravada Buddhism, from which it spread through much of Southeast Asia. Buddhism's transformation from an Indian regional tradition to a pan-Asian religion is largely attributable to Ashoka's patronage and outreach.

The [[event:1102|Decline of Maurya Empire]] after Ashoka's death in 232 BC suggests that his moral governance was not matched by political effectiveness in ensuring succession and maintaining centralized control. But his legacy endures: he is the model of the Buddhist ruler, his wheel of dharma (Dharmachakra) appears on India's national flag, and he remains for many the most admirable ruler antiquity produced.
